answer:
Across the four volumes of the Traité de Législation, Comte's argument moves through three distinct phases:

**Volumes I–II and III (abstract/comparative foundation):** Volume I establishes the methodological and theoretical framework — critiquing prior systems (natural law, social contract, general will, utility) and building a positive account of what law is and how it works. Volume II shifts to comparative anthropology: the physical, intellectual, and moral development of different human species across climates (copper-colored, Malay, Negro, Mongol), plus their social organization. Volume III continues this comparative survey through Mongol and Caucasian peoples, then analyzes how local circumstances shape peoples' faculties. Slavery appears in this section only incidentally, as a comparative term.

**First pivot — the argument becomes specifically about slavery at Vol III, Chapter 49** (slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iii-ca589a, ch. 49, 'Du penchant à la servitude et de quelques autres vices attribués aux peuples d'espèces colorées'). Here Comte directly takes up the claim that colored peoples have a natural 'penchant for servitude' and systematically dismantles it. This chapter is still comparative and theoretical in mode — it argues that if servitude were natural to a species, all members would equally tend toward it, making slavery impossible — but it is now arguing *about* slavery specifically, not merely using it as a comparative term.

**Second pivot — the argument becomes the dedicated book on domestic slavery at Vol IV, Chapter 1** (slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040, ch. 1). Vol IV opens with a chapter explicitly framing 'domestic slavery' as the subject of the entire volume, distinct from the 'political slavery' analyzed in earlier books. Chapters 2–5 of Vol IV remain abstract and general: Ch. 2 defines the types of domestic slavery; Ch. 3 analyzes its effects on the physical constitution of masters and slaves; Ch. 4 on intellectual faculties; Ch. 5 on the free industrious population. These chapters reason from general principles, not from particular cases.

**The argument leaves the abstract for historical case studies at Vol IV, Chapter 6** (slug: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-iv-bca040, ch. 6, 'De l'influence de l'esclavage sur les mœurs des Romains'). This is the first chapter dedicated entirely to a named, historical society. Comte explicitly opens Ch. 6 by noting that, for ancient slavery, Rome is the only people about whom sufficient records survive to make judgments. Historical case studies then continue through Ch. 7 (British colonies), Ch. 8 (French colonies), Ch. 9 (United States), Ch. 10 (French colonies continued), and Ch. 11 (South American peoples).

In summary: the argument becomes specifically about slavery at Vol III Ch. 49 (as a rebuttal of natural-servitude claims in the comparative anthropology) and definitively at Vol IV Ch. 1 (as the subject of an entire book). It leaves the abstract for historical case studies at Vol IV Ch. 6, with Rome as the first named case.

tool_calls.name: list_works
tool_calls.result_summary: Found all four volumes of Traité de Législation (Vol I: charles-comte-traite-de-legislation-vol-i-7e62a2; Vol II: -vol-ii-20331d; Vol III: -vol-iii-ca589a; Vol IV: -vol-iv-bca040), plus two volumes of Traité de la propriété

tool_calls.name: list_chapters
tool_calls.result_summary: 22 chapters: abstract methodology (chs 1-10), elements of law (chs 11-21), preface (ch 22)

tool_calls.name: list_chapters
tool_calls.result_summary: 27 chapters: comparative anthropology of human species — physical/intellectual/moral development, ending with Negro peoples (chs 25-27)

tool_calls.name: list_chapters
tool_calls.result_summary: 23 chapters (numbered 28-50): Mongol and Caucasian peoples, local circumstances, culminating in ch. 49 on 'penchant à la servitude' and ch. 50 as conclusion

tool_calls.name: list_chapters
tool_calls.result_summary: 23 chapters (numbered 1-23): entire volume on domestic slavery — chs 1-5 abstract, ch 6 Rome, chs 7-11 modern colonies, chs 12-23 systemic effects and abolition

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Ch. 49 argues against the claim that colored peoples have a natural penchant for servitude; uses comparative/theoretical reasoning plus empirical examples (Louisiana, Saint-Domingue, Carolina, Massachusetts). First chapter specifically about slavery, still in comparative mode.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Ch. 1 of Vol IV introduces domestic slavery as the explicit subject of the entire book, contrasts it with political slavery, critiques current laws dividing humans into 'persons' and 'things', and motivates the study. Still abstract/definitional.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Ch. 2 defines the types of domestic slavery and how it arises from conquest. Abstract/analytical.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Ch. 3 analyzes influence of slavery on physical constitution — still general/theoretical.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Ch. 6 is explicitly titled 'On the influence of slavery on the morals of the Romans' — Rome is the first named historical case study. Opens by noting Rome is the only ancient case documented well enough to analyze.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Ch. 50 concludes Vol III by still arguing about racial causes of European progress — comparative/theoretical, not a slavery case study.
